coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots modified dual-vacancy Z-scheme CuFe2O4/g-C3N4 photocatalyst synergistic with Fenton technique for photothermal degradation of antibiotics

Bai, Haina
coredata.dc:description: Photo-Fenton technology has emerged as a promising solution for treating polluted wastewater, but developing photocatalysts with high photocatalytic activity is often challenging. Through the coupling of two semiconductors with good band gaps, oxygen vacancy CuFe2O4/nitrogen vacancy g-C3N4 (CFOv/NvCN) composites modified by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were designed to synergistically couple the Fenton technique for the degradation of antibiotics. The N-CQDs serve as effective conductors bridging between CFOv and NvCN, facilitating highly efficient processes of spatial charge separation and carrier transfer. Furthermore, the electron transfer pathways were investigated using density functional theory (DFT). Benefiting from the Z-scheme charge transfer channel, electrons migrate from NvCN through N-CQDs to CFOv, thereby preserving high reducibility (e−) and high oxidizability (h+). Additionally, it was observed that defect engineering and photothermal effects further accelerate the rate of electron transfer. The experimental results indicate that N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN achieved an 85.69 % degradation efficiency of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) within 60 min. This study further utilized three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (3D EEMs) and two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2D COS) to investigate the organic degradation dynamics during the TC degradation process and assess the ecotoxicity of intermediate products. Drawing upon both experimental results and density functional theory, a proposed degradation mechanism for TC has been presented, offering novel insights into the application of photocatalysts in wastewater treatment.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots modified dual-vacancy Z-scheme CuFe2O4/g-C3N4 photocatalyst synergistic with Fenton technique for photothermal degradation of antibiotics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-Fenton technology has emerged as a promising solution for treating polluted wastewater, but developing photocatalysts with high photocatalytic activity is often challenging. Through the coupling of two semiconductors with good band gaps, oxygen vacancy CuFe2O4/nitrogen vacancy g-C3N4 (CFOv/NvCN) composites modified by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were designed to synergistically couple the Fenton technique for the degradation of antibiotics. The N-CQDs serve as effective conductors bridging between CFOv and NvCN, facilitating highly efficient processes of spatial charge separation and carrier transfer. Furthermore, the electron transfer pathways were investigated using density functional theory (DFT). Benefiting from the Z-scheme charge transfer channel, electrons migrate from NvCN through N-CQDs to CFOv, thereby preserving high reducibility (e−) and high oxidizability (h+). Additionally, it was observed that defect engineering and photothermal effects further accelerate the rate of electron transfer. The experimental results indicate that N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN achieved an 85.69 % degradation efficiency of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) within 60 min. This study further utilized three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (3D EEMs) and two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2D COS) to investigate the organic degradation dynamics during the TC degradation process and assess the ecotoxicity of intermediate products. Drawing upon both experimental results and density functional theory, a proposed degradation mechanism for TC has been presented, offering novel insights into the application of photocatalysts in wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the number of antibiotics detected in the environment has continued to increase and has been regarded as the number one threat to human health [1]. Due to the poor biodegradability of antibiotics, conventional water treatment methods cannot effectively remove them. In addition, the long-term presence of antibiotics will promote the development of antibiotic resistance in bacteria [2–6]. These resistant bacteria will further spread to humans and animals, increasing the difficulty of treating infectious diseases and exacerbating the global problem of antibiotic resistance. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop an efficient, stable, environmentally friendly, and economical antibiotic wastewater treatment technology. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have garnered widespread attention due to their high efficiency in significantly degrading and mineralizing toxic organic pollutants dissolved in water, including various antibiotics, phenolic compounds, and emerging contaminants [7,8,10–12]. In replacing traditional water treatment methods, advanced oxidation technologies based on H2O2, such as Fenton and Fenton-like reactions, utilize Fe2+ to activate H2O2, thereby generating reactive species with strong oxidative capabilities towards wastewater [13]. However, traditional Fenton technology suffers from certain drawbacks, including a narrow pH range, low H2O2 utilization efficiency, and the generation of considerable amounts of metal precipitates, thereby limiting its applicability [14]. Therefore, the combination of Fenton technology with photocatalysis, known as photocatalytic Fenton technology, has attracted widespread attention [15]. In photocatalytic Fenton technology, the generated electrons from the material can promote H2O2 decomposition and facilitate Fe2+ regeneration. Due to its reduced sensitivity to pH, photocatalytic Fenton technology holds promise for application over a wider pH range. In order to effectively remove antibiotics from water, it is of great significance to develop photocatalysts with high catalytic activity.

Carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as a graphite-phase semiconductor with visible light responsiveness, is commonly utilized in the field of photocatalysis due to its high catalytic activity, low cost, and ease of synthesis [16]. However, pure g-C3N4 exhibits unsatisfactory catalytic performance in the field of photocatalytic Fenton due to drawbacks such as rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and insufficient absorption of visible light [17,18]. Therefore, modification methods such as morphology engineering [19], elemental doping [20], heterostructure construction [21], and defect engineering [22] are widely employed to enhance the photocatalytic Fenton activity of g-C3N4 [23]. Previous studies have shown that introducing defects into g-C3N4 can improve its optoelectronic properties [24] and broaden its absorption range in the visible light spectrum [25]. However, single-component materials still face challenges such as short carrier lifetimes and inadequate redox capabilities.

Currently, heterostructure construction is regarded as the most promising material modification strategy [26]. Traditional type I or type II heterojunctions facilitate the separation of photogenerated charges but weaken the material's redox capability. Z-scheme photocatalysts and other types of heterojunctions, such as S-scheme heterojunctions [27], can achieve strong redox reactions and spatial charge separation, thereby promoting photocatalytic reactions [28]. In traditional Z-type photocatalysts, reversible redox ion pairs serve as the transport medium for charge carriers. Although Z-type heterojunctions have some advantages in improving photocatalytic performance, recombination effects in the heterojunction structure remain a challenge when electrons are clustered at the band-edge positions [29]. Effective inhibition of photogenerated carrier complexation by loading co-catalysts on photocatalysts [30]. N-CQDs exhibit remarkable electron transfer and storage capabilities and are commonly used as an alternative to noble metals for surface modification of other catalysts. Complexation with other semiconductors promotes interfacial charge carrier mobility, prolongs the charge lifetime, and is therefore regarded as an ideal material for the modification of heterojunctions [31]. Copper ferrite (CuFe2O4) is a typical spinel ferrite that has garnered attention in recent years due to its advantageous properties, such as ferromagnetism, which facilitates catalyst recovery, and excellent visible light responsiveness [32]. Additionally, the higher photothermal performance of CuFe2O4 enables the conversion of visible light into thermal energy, thereby synergistically enhancing the photocatalytic performance of photocatalytic Fenton processes [33]. It has been reported that the introduction of oxygen vacancies into CuFe2O4 can improve the absorption and utilization of visible light and promote the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance.

In this study, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composites were prepared and used as photo-Fenton catalysts to degrade tetracycline hydrochloride (TC). The aim of this study is as follows: (i) Synthesize N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN photocatalysts via facile thermal polymerization and in-situ deposition methods; (ii) The microstructure and electron transfer pathways of the materials were elucidated through experimental characterization and theoretical calculations; (iii) Investigating the influence of Z-type heterojunctions on the photocatalytic degradation performance. (iv) Exploring the degradation dynamics of different organic compounds during the TC degradation process through 3D EEMs and 2D COS analysis; (v) elucidating the degradation pathways of TC and assessing the toxicity of degradation intermediates.
Detailed information on the reagents and materials utilized in this investigation can be found in Supplementary Text S1.

NvCN was prepared using a straightforward thermal polymerization method [14]. Initially, 4.45 mmol of KOH was solubilized in 30 mL of DI water. Following this, 15 g of melamine was incrementally introduced into the previously mentioned solution, with stirring maintained for 15 min to ensure complete and homogeneous mixing. The mixture was then dried thoroughly at 75 °C in an oven and transferred to a crucible. It was then heated to 600 °C at a ramp rate of 3 °C/min and maintained at this temperature for 2 h. The resulting product was denoted as NvCN.

Disperse 3.5 mmol of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and 7.0 mmol of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O in 70 mL of deionized water along with 10.5 mmol of citric acid under ultrasonication at 75 °C for 2 h. Then, maintain the mixture at 125 °C in an oven for 14 h. Subsequently, calcine the mixture in air at a ramp rate of 5 °C/min to 400 °C and hold it at this temperature for 2 h. The resulting product is denoted as CFOv.

The binary composite material CFOv/NvCN was synthesized via a thermal polymerization method. Different mass ratios of NvCN and CFOv were placed in a crucible, and 20 mL of deionized water was added. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonication for 3 h to ensure thorough dispersion. After drying thoroughly in an 80 °C oven, the mixture was ground into powder. The resulting powder was then heated in a muffle furnace at a ramp rate of 5 °C/min to 300 °C and maintained for 2 h. The obtained products were labeled according to the mass ratio of CFOv to NvCN as CFOv/NvCN(1:5), CFOv/NvCN(1:4), CFOv/NvCN(1:3), CFOv/NvCN(1:2), CFOv/NvCN(1:1), and CFOv/NvCN(2:1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs were synthesized via an electrochemical etching method. Two parallel graphite rods were used as the cathode and anode. The electrolyte solution consisted of 40 mL of 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution, 20 mL of 0.1 mol/L NH3·H2O, and 20 mL of ultrapure water. Electrochemical etching was performed at a constant voltage of 20 V for 2 h. Subsequently, the resulting dark brown solution was filtered three times through double-layer filter paper. After drying, the powder product of N-CQDs was obtained.

N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN were synthesized via an in-situ deposition method. A certain mass of N-CQDs and 0.1 g of CFOv/NvCN were placed in a crucible, and 20 mL of deionized water was added. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonication for 6 h to ensure thorough dispersion. After drying thoroughly in an 80 °C oven, a series of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalysts were prepared. The mass content of CQDs varied as 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 mg, labeled respectively as N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN-4, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN-6, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN-8, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN-10, and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN-12 composite catalysts.

To investigate the photocatalytic activity of catalysts, a certain mass of catalyst was dispersed in a solution containing 100 mL of TC (tetracycline) at a concentration of 10 mg/L. The dispersion was stirred under dark conditions for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. A 300 W xenon lamp was used as the light source, which provided an irradiance of ∼ 170 W m−2, measured at the surface of the working solution by a Thorlabs PM100D luminometer. Add 1 mM H2O2 to the system to activate the Fenton-like reaction. During the illumination process, 4 mL suspension samples were taken every 5 min for analysis. The concentration of tetracycline was determined using a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system (Prominence-I LC-2030 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan). The HPLC system was equipped with a C18 chromatographic column (4.6 mm × 150 mm, 5 μm), maintained at a column temperature of 25 °C. The mobile phase consisted of acetic acid (1 ‰) and acetonitrile in a ratio of 90:10. The flow rate was set at 1 mL/min, and the detection wavelength was set at 357 nm. An injection volume of 10 μL was used for analysis.
The analysis methods for TC concentration, detailed density functional theory (DFT) calculations, and comprehensive characterization information are provided in Supplementary Text S2.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of NvCN, CFOv, CFOv/NvCN, and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN are shown in Fig. 1 a. The original CFOv exhibits a series of characteristic peaks well matched with the standard card of spinel-phase CFO (JCPDS 77–0010), with 2θ values at 30.16°, 35.52°, 43.2°, 53.62°, 57.14°, 62.74°, and 74.25° corresponding to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), and (533) crystal planes of CFO, respectively [34]. Diffraction peaks at 13.34° and 27.6° correspond to the conjugated structure (100) and the interface stacking of the g-C3N4 and heptazine networks (002) (JCPDS No. 87–1526), respectively. In the CFOv/NvCN and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN systems, the (100) diffraction peak of NvCN nearly disappears; however, the diffraction peaks of both CFOv and NvCN are still detectable, confirming the coexistence of CFOv and NvCN.

The FT-IR spectra of the prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 1b. For NvCN, peaks observed in the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 (at 1238, 1315, 1457, 1573, and 1633 cm−1) are consistent with the presence of C-N heterocyclic groups [35–37], while the broad band centered at 3150 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching mode of N-H bonds [38], and the peak at 808 cm−1 corresponds to the triazine groups of g-C3N4 [39]. In CFOv, the vibration band at 547 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of metal oxide ν (M−O), characteristic of copper ferrite [40], while the characteristic absorption band at 419 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration mode of octahedral Cu-O in the spinel structure of CFOv [39]. The spectra of N-CQDs exhibit peaks at 1402 and 3443 cm−1, representing O-H and C-O-C bonds in N-CQDs [41]. In both CFOv/NvCN and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, characteristic peaks of CFOv and NvCN are observed, while no characteristic peaks of N-CQDs are observed in the ternary composite materials, possibly due to the low concentration of N-CQDs. These results indicate that the graphitic structure of NvCN is well preserved upon the introduction of N-CQDs and CFOv.

Considering that the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs and the introduction of CFOv may affect the surface properties of NvCN, the water contact angles of NvCN, CFOv, and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN were tested. As shown in Fig. S1 (a-b), the hydrophilicity of NvCN was improved after the introduction of CFOv. Fig. S1c shows that after the introduction of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN exhibited the strongest hydrophilicity, which may be due to the fact that the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs promote the interaction between the photocatalyst and water. The enhanced hydrophilicity helps to increase the contact area between the catalyst and the reactants in water, thereby enhancing the efficiency of the catalytic reaction [42].

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy tests were conducted to identify the introduction of vacancies in the material. The results presented in Fig. 1 (c-d), reveal signal intensity at g = 2.004 for CFOv, indicating the presence of oxygen vacancies. Similarly, NvCN exhibits signal intensity at g = 2.004, indicating the existence of nitrogen vacancies within NvCN. Notably, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN display a higher signal intensity compared to CFOv and NvCN, indicative of a greater concentration of unpaired electrons [43]. This observed phenomenon may be attributed to the simultaneous presence of nitrogen and oxygen vacancies in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, leading to variations in peak intensity due to the dual vacancy effect.

The distribution of active sites and the adsorption capacity of catalysts depend on their specific surface area and pore structure, which are important factors influencing photocatalytic capability. From the results in Fig. S2a, it can be observed that the adsorption–desorption isotherm of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN follows type IV and exhibits a typical H3 hysteresis loop, indicating a typical mesoporous structure of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. Additionally, in Fig. S2b, the average pore diameter of N-CQDs, CFOv, and NvCN is determined to be 24.9888 nm. The BET surface area of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN is 17.4122 m2/g, and this larger specific surface area not only facilitates the adsorption of TC but also provides more reaction sites for the catalytic process.

Through XPS analysis, the chemical elemental composition and relevant valence states of the samples were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, C, N, O, Cu, and Fe are coexistent in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. The C 1 s spectrum of the catalysts in Fig. 2b reveals three characteristic peaks corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-O, and N-C-N bonds for both NvCN and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. Fig. 2c displays the N 1 s spectra of NvCN and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, where the peaks at 398.35 eV, 399.13 eV, 400.77 eV, and 404.36 eV for N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN correspond to C-N=C(N1), (C)3-N(N2), N-H(N3), and π-π* states, respectively. The ratio of N1/(N2 + N3) in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN (1.7753) is higher than that in NvCN (1.0283), as shown in Fig. S3, indicating the presence of more hybridized nitrogen in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, affirming the formation of heterogeneous structures and enhancing the interface interaction between N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN [44]. Moreover, the increased N1 content contributes to lowering the local work function, providing more Lewis basic sites, promoting O2 adsorption, and thus improving catalytic activity [45]. The O 1 s spectra of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN in Fig. 2d exhibit three peaks at 529.62 eV, 530.21 eV, and 531.71 eV, corresponding to the existence of metal oxygen (M−O), oxygen vacancies (Ov), and O-H groups [46]. Additionally, the peak area representing O-H in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN significantly increases from 16.08 % in NvCN to 60.60 %, indirectly confirming the successful loading of N-CQDs into the material [47]. The Fe 2p spectrum of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN in Fig. 2e displays two distinct spin–orbit doublets corresponding to Fe 2p 1/2 and Fe 2p 3/2. Peaks at 710.71 eV and 723.53 eV are attributed to Fe2+, while those at 713.29 eV and 726.09 eV correspond to Fe3+. These findings suggest the presence of both Fe2+ and Fe3+ within the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composite. Additionally, the two broad peaks at 718.70 eV and 731.50 eV are generated by satellite peaks. In Fig. 2f, the Cu 2p spectrum of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN shows peaks at 932.33 eV and 933.84 eV corresponding to Cu+ and Cu2+, indicating the coexistence of Cu+ and Cu2+ in the material. Peaks at 941.37 eV and 943.73 eV are attributed to satellite peaks. The coexistence of metal ions in different oxidation states promotes the electron transfer process on the catalyst surface, thereby facilitating the oxidation–reduction cycle of H2O2 activation [47].

Through SEM analysis, the apparent morphology of NvCN, NvCN/CFOv, and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN was explored, as shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3a, the original NvCN presents a typical layered structure, while in Fig. 3b, CFOv exists in the form of stacked small particles consisting of many unit particles, which is consistent with the morphology of pure CFOv in Fig. S4. CFOv is loaded on the surface of the NvCN layer, increasing the specific surface area of the reaction and providing more active sites for the reaction [48]. In Fig. 3c, it is shown that the surface of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN becomes rougher after loading N-CQDs [29], indicating N-CQDs are attached to the catalyst surface. In Fig. 3(d-j), the EDX spectra demonstrate the presence of elements C, N, O, K, Fe, and Cu in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, with a uniform distribution of elements, further confirming the successful preparation of the composite sample. The EDS spectrum shows the mass percentage of elements contained in the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composite material (Fig. 3k).

TEM was utilized to further investigate the microstructure characteristics of the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalyst. From Fig. 4 (a-b) that the CFOv and NvCN structures are coupled together, and the N-CQDs nanoparticles are attached to the catalyst surface. The N-CQDs show a slight aggregation tendency, and the particle size is mainly distributed in the range of less than 10 nm, with an average particle size of 8.18 nm, as shown in the inset histogram. The TEM image of pure N-CQDs is shown in Fig. S5, and it can be seen that its average particle size is 8.30 nm, which is consistent with the average particle size of N-CQDs in the composite material. Therefore, the close interfacial contact formed between NvCN, CFOv, and N-CQDs is expected to facilitate charge transfer during subsequent catalytic processes, thereby enhancing catalytic performance. These results indicate that the N-CQDs/ CFOv/NvCN composites have been successfully prepared.

Generally, catalysts with photothermal conversion capability can enhance the catalytic activity of the photo-Fenton reaction by improving intrinsic activity. Introducing moderate heat into the photo-Fenton reaction may be an effective strategy to enhance efficiency. Therefore, the photothermal performance of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN in water under 300 W xenon lamp irradiation for 30 min was investigated, and temperature fluctuations were recorded using a non-contact thermal infrared camera. Fig. 5 a records the photothermal conversioncapability of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN in the photo-Fenton system. Upon addition of 0.15 g/L N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN to the TC solution, the temperature increased from an initial temperature of 25.6 °C to 37.7 °C within 30 min. Compared to pure water (Fig. S6), which increased from 25.7 °C to 32.4 °C under 30 min of light irradiation, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN achieved nearly a twofold increase in temperature. The higher temperature change is advantageous for enhancing catalytic activity, which has a positive impact on improving degradation efficiency. The effect of the photothermal effect of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalyst on TC degradation in the photo-Fenton system was studied in Fig. S7. It can be observed from the figure that higher temperatures are conducive to the improvement of catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 5b, the enhancement of catalytic activity due to the photothermal effect may be attributed to the generation of “hot electrons” in the photothermal material. These “hot electrons” can facilitate the transfer of electrons in the reaction, thereby aiding in increasing the reaction rate [49]. Moreover, these “hot electrons” possess higher energy and can participate in redox catalytic reactions before releasing heat to form thermal energy. They can accelerate the cycling of Fe3+/Fe2+ and Cu2+/Cu+ in CFOv, thereby promoting the progress of the photo-Fenton reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of TC on different systems
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the optimal vacancy content, the addition amounts of KOH and citric acid were optimized. As shown in Fig. S8, CFOv and NvCN prepared when the addition amount of citric acid was 10.5 mmol and the addition amount of KOH was 4.45 mmol had the best photocatalytic activity. The catalytic activity of the prepared catalysts for the removal of TC in the photo-Fenton system was investigated. Then, the effects of different mass ratios of binary and ternary catalysts on the degradation of TC during the photo-Fenton degradation process were investigated. Fig. S9 (a-b) shows the variation of TC degradation efficiency by binary and ternary catalysts under different CFOv and N-CQDs contents. The results showed that the degradation efficiency showed a gradual increase and then decreased with the increase in CFOv and N-CQDs content. In binary catalyst systems, a judicious addition of CFOv enhances the active sites on the NvCN surface, mitigating carrier complexation. However, excessive CFOv content leads to agglomeration on the NvCN surface, diminishing light absorption efficiency. In the prepared samples, CFOv/NvCN (1:2) exhibited the highest TC degradation efficiency of 64.05 % after 30 min, which was 16.77 % higher compared to NvCN (47.28 %) and 16.22 % higher compared to CFOv. The first-order reaction rate constant of 0.0293 min−1 for CFOv/NvCN (1:2) was 1.38 and 1.66 times higher than that of NvCN and CFOv. The N-CQDs/NvCN and N-CQDs/CFOv binary nanocomposites constructed by introducing N-CQDs into NvCN and CFOv showed higher degradation efficiencies of 66.79 % and 63.75 % due to the improved charge separation efficiency. The construction of ternary heterojunction N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN can further improve the catalytic activity. When the mass ratio of N-CQDs is 8 %, the degradation efficiency of TC is 72.98 %. As the concentration of N-CQDs increases, the degradation efficiency decreases, which can be attributed to the competition for photon absorption induced by high levels of N-CQDs [50]. Consequently, the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN-8 catalyst was selected for subsequent research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of parameters on TC removal by N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to explore the optimal experimental parameters of the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN system, the effects of different factors on TC degradation were studied. First, the effect of catalyst dosage on TC degradation efficiency was explored. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, an escalation in catalyst dosage from 5 mg to 15.0 mg resulted in a notable enhancement in degradation efficiency, rising from 45.58 % to 76.53 %. Additionally, the associated pseudo-first-order reaction rate and constant exhibited a proportional increase, ascending from 0.0189 min−1 at 5 mg catalyst dosage to 0.043 min−1 at 15.0 mg catalyst dosage. The above results show that moderately increasing the catalyst dosage can increase more reaction sites [39], stimulate more electrons, and promote the consumption of H2O2 under the action of light, thereby improving the removal efficiency of TC. As the dosage of the catalyst continues to increase, the degradation rate exhibits a decreasing trend, and the corresponding reaction rate constants also decrease. The reason for the above phenomenon may be twofold. On the one hand, excessive introduction of the catalyst may lead to a significant increase in active sites, causing quenching of the generated ·OH radicals, thereby reducing the concentration of active species in the system (Eq. (1) [51]. On the other hand, excessive addition of the catalyst may result in uneven dispersion in the solution, leading to aggregation phenomena and subsequent turbidity and light scattering, thereby weakening light transmission and reducing the utilization efficiency of the photo-Fenton system.
(1)

The influence of varying TC concentrations on the degradation efficiency of TC using N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN was examined, as illustrated in Fig. 6b. As the TC concentration rose from 5 mg/L to 10 mg/L, the elimination efficacy of TC within 30 min escalated from 69.09 % to 76.47 %, coinciding with an increase in the corresponding first-order kinetic rate constant from 0.0292 min−1 to 0.0402 min−1. Nevertheless, with subsequent increments in TC concentration, reaching 30 mg/L, the removal efficiency dwindled to 35.51 %. This indicates a negative influence of higher TC concentrations on the removal efficiency. The reduction in efficiency could be attributed to the restricted availability of active species produced by the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN/Vis/H2O2 system. Under high TC concentration conditions, more intermediate products may be generated, competing with TC molecules for the limited active sites, thus failing to adequately react with excessive pollutants [52].

The concentration of H2O2 is a crucial factor influencing photodegradation in the photo-Fenton process. Fig. 6c illustrates the efficiency of TC removal by the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composite material under different H2O2 concentrations. With the gradual increase in H2O2 concentration from 0.5 mM to 10 mM, the photodegradation efficiency of TC reached 66.06 %, 76.47 %, 77.98 %, and 85.69 %, respectively, indicating that an appropriate H2O2 concentration is conducive to the formation of highly active free radicals. Nevertheless, upon further increases in H2O2 concentration, there was no significant improvement in the degradation efficiency, indicating that the additional increment in H2O2 concentration does not notably enhance the photocatalytic activity of the photo-Fenton reaction for TC. When increased to 20 mM, the degradation efficiency of TC was 88.75 %, with only a 4.44 % increase compared to a 10 mM H2O2 concentration, indicating an insignificant improvement in efficiency. This minor enhancement in efficiency may be attributed to the excess H2O2 capturing ·OH species in the reaction system, thereby impeding the photo-Fenton degradation reaction (Eq. (2). Therefore, to avoid additional treatment costs, the ideal H2O2 concentration should be 10 mM.

To further explore the application potential of the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN photo-Fenton system, compared with other reports (Table S1) [53–57], N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN achieved higher TC removal efficiency at lower catalyst dosage and illumination time, indicating excellent catalytic performance.

The performance of traditional Fenton reactions is largely influenced by the pH of the reaction solution, yet the restricted pH adaptability limits its practical application potential in different environments [58]. The influence of different pH values on performance was explored under fixed reaction conditions, as described earlier. As shown in Fig. 6d, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN exhibited optimal photo-Fenton catalytic activity at an initial pH value of 4.68. The degradation efficiencies at different pH values (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) were 77.67 %, 79.48 %, 82.92 %, 84.28 %, 82.69 %, and 81.38 %, respectively. Traditional Fenton reactions typically exhibit good adaptability within a pH range of 3–5 [59], whereas the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalyst demonstrated degradation efficiencies exceeding 77 % for TC across a pH range of 1 to 11. Although degradation efficiencies showed a varying degree of decline when the solution was acidic or alkaline, overall, the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalyst exhibited relatively high catalytic activity over a wider pH range.

To further explain the decline in photo-Fenton activity, the pH drift technique was used to ascertain the surface charge and zero point of charge (pH pzc) of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. According to the results in inset of Fig. 6d, the pH pzc of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN was determined to be 7.52. This indicates that at pH<7.52, the surface of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN carries a positive charge, while it becomes negatively charged when pH>7.52. TC is an amphoteric substance (pKa = 3.3, 7.7, 9.7), and its existence forms are influenced by the solution pH [60,61]. Specifically, under acidic, neutral, and alkaline conditions, TC mainly exists in the forms of positively charged (TC+), molecular (TC0), and negatively charged (TC−), respectively. In acidic environments, the degradation efficiency decreases due to the repulsion between the positively charged surfaces of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN and TC+. In the pH range of 5–9, the repulsion effect between the positive charge of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN and the neutral TC0 is weakened, explaining the slight decrease in degradation efficiency observed at pH 5–7. At pH 11, the surface of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN becomes negatively charged, leading to repulsion with TC in its negatively charged form, thereby reducing the photo-Fenton activity.

Additionally, the reaction pathways of free radicals in the system differ at different pH values [1]. In acidic solutions (pH=1, 3), the introduction of Cl− has a scavenging effect on ·OH, thereby hindering the Fenton reaction (Eq. (3) [62]. In alkaline solutions (pH=11), the instability of H2O2 leads to its decomposition into H2O and O2 (Eq. (4). Furthermore, excessive alkaline conditions can generate relatively weakly active iron species (Eq. (5), reducing the activity of H2O2. Higher-valence iron species may also be generated in alkaline solutions, which are less active than ·OH [63], further hindering TC degradation.

These results indicate that the prepared N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN heterojunction helps maintain the stability of iron species and promotes the oxidation–reduction cycle of Fe2+/Fe3+, making it adaptable to a wide range of pH values. This enhancement is of positive significance for expanding its application potential in practical fields [64].

This study examined the influence of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN on the degradation of the pollutant TC across various systems. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composite exhibited removal efficiencies of 44.97 % and 49.85 % for TC in the photocatalytic and Fenton systems, respectively. Notably, in the photo-Fenton system, it achieved an enhanced removal efficiency of 85.69 %. This suggests a synergistic interplay between photocatalysis and the Fenton reaction, where light notably amplifies electron transfer and catalyzes the Fenton process, thus substantially enhancing degradation efficiency. In this study, the photocatalytic (kp) and Fenton (kF) degradation rates have been determined based on their synergistic effects, as shown in Eq. (6).
(6)
Synergy index =
k PF
k P
+
k F

Based on the information presented in Table S2, the calculated synergistic index for the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composite material is 1.565 (>1), signifying a substantial synergistic interplay between photocatalysis and the Fenton reaction, thereby enhancing the degradation efficiency.

The study examined the influence of various water matrices on the degradation of TC. Investigations involved the use of tap water and water sourced from the Songhua River to evaluate TC degradation efficiency across different aqueous environments. The results, as shown in Fig. 7b, indicate that in tap water and water from the Songhua River, the degradation efficiency of TC was 84.18 % and 84.36 %, respectively, only slightly lower than the removal efficiency in deionized water by 1.51 % and 1.33 %, respectively. The pH values detected for tap water and Songhua River water are approximately 6.8 and range from 7.0 to 8.0, respectively. Based on the preceding discussion regarding pH values, it can be inferred that these variations may exert a minor influence on the degradation efficiency of TC. The slight decrease in TC degradation efficiency observed in natural water bodies can be attributed to the presence of Cl− ions in tap water and the potential presence of organic substances such as humic acids in the Songhua River water. These entities compete with H2O2 for active sites, thereby inhibiting the degradation of tetracycline by the photo-Fenton system and consequently reducing the efficiency of the photo-Fenton process [38,64]. The experimental results demonstrate the significant potential of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN in water treatment applications.

Furthermore, this study investigated the impact of different oxidants (persulfate, peroxymonosulfate, and H2O2) on the reactive activity exhibited during the oxidation process of TC. As shown in Fig. 7c, the addition of 10 mM H2O2, PS, and PMS resulted in degradation efficiencies of 85.69 %, 97.83 %, and 99.19 % for TC, respectively, after 30 min. However, the use of PS and PMS in the photo-Fenton degradation of TC may lead to the formation of harmful SO4 2−, which could potentially cause secondary pollution in water bodies. For environmental sustainability, H2O2 was utilized as the oxidizing agent in the photo-Fenton degradation process of TC.

In practical applications, various inorganic salt ions commonly present in real water bodies may interfere with the removal process of target pollutants. Fig. 7 (d-e) illustrates the effects of anions (Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, and CO3 2−) and cations (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and K+) on the photo-Fenton degradation of TC. It is observed that, the addition of 10 mM CO3 2− had no significant impact on the catalytic activity of the catalyst, with removal efficiency still maintained at above 85 %. The addition of 10 mM Cl− showed a slight negative effect on the degradation of TC, which could be attributed to both the adsorption of Cl− [65], potentially occupying active sites on the catalyst surface, and the inhibition caused by ·Cl− generation, as shown in Eq. (7) [66]. The introduction of 10 mM SO4 2− also exhibited an inhibitory effect on the photo-Fenton degradation of TC, likely due to the generation of the low-active free radical ·SO4 −, as depicted in Eq. (8). The inhibition of NO3 − on TC degradation can be attributed to its light absorption, leading to filtration under sunlight in the range of 290–370 nm, and the hindrance of the photo-Fenton reaction by the generation of ·NO3, as described in Eq. (9) [67]. Regarding cations, all showed slight inhibitory effects on TC degradation. The inhibition of TC degradation by cations ranked from highest to lowest as Mg2+ > Ca2+ > Na+ > K+. This inhibition may occur because cations compete with Fe2+ for active sites on the catalyst, and high ionic strength can cause catalyst aggregation, thereby negatively affecting TC degradation [68].

In order to further investigate the versatility of the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalyst, experiments were conducted on the photo-Fenton degradation of various pollutants. Fig. 7f illustrates the degradation of different pollutants by the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalyst. For organic dyes, the photo-Fenton degradation efficiency of crystal violet reached a maximum of 98.87 %. The degradation efficiencies of other dyes such as Congo red, rhodamine 6G, and reactive black 5 also exceeded 80 %, with values of 93.48 %, 81.57 %, and 81.19 %, respectively. Regarding antibiotics, the degradation efficiencies of norfloxacin and tetracycline were 72.84 % and 68.53 %, respectively. These experimental results indicate that the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN catalyst prepared in this study exhibits excellent universality in the degradation of various pollutants.

The catalyst's reusability and stability are crucial factors determining its practical application. The magnetic properties of CFOv and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN were analyzed using Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM) technique. Fig. S10a displays the magnetic hysteresis loops of the catalyst within the magnetic field range from −10 kG to + 10 kG. The S-shaped hysteresis loops, nonlinearity, and reversibility of the curves indicate the ferromagnetic nature of the catalyst [69]. The magnetic parameters of CFOv and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, including saturation magnetization (Ms), remanent magnetization (Mr), and coercivity field (Hc), are listed in the Table S3. The coercivity field (Hc) values of CFOv and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN are approximately 175.335 and 150.735 G, respectively, with remanence values of 5.617 and 1.650 emu/g, confirming the prepared catalysts as typical soft magnetic materials with low coercivity and remanence. The saturation magnetization values of CFOv and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN are 25.859 and 8.095 emu/g, respectively, with the decrease in saturation magnetization of the composite sample attributed to the introduction of N-CQDs and NvCN. However, the illustration demonstrates that under the influence of an external magnetic field, the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composite material can be completely separated and recovered from the dispersion liquid within a short time. This indicates the excellent magnetic responsiveness and efficient magnetic recovery performance of the composite material, thus reducing material loss and potential secondary pollution risks in practical applications.

To assess the stability of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, five consecutive photocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted for TC. After each degradation cycle, the catalyst was collected from the reaction suspension using a magnet, washed, and dried for subsequent recycling experiments. As depicted in Fig. S10b, the removal efficiency of TC was 85.69 %, 84.75 %, 83.24 %, 82.11 %, and 79.12 % for each cycle, respectively. After five cycles, the removal efficiency of TC decreased by only 6.57 %, indicating that the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composite material maintained relatively stable degradation performance over five cycles, confirming its excellent chemical stability and potential feasibility for industrial applications.

In Fig. S10 (c-f), it is observed that the XRD, FT-IR, XPS, and UV–Vis spectra of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN show little to no significant changes before and after use. This suggests that the crystal phase, structure, and photochemical properties of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN remain highly stable. The TEM image of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN after use is shown in Fig. S11. It was found that the morphology of the sample after use did not change significantly, indicating that N-CQDs were in situ deposited on CFOv/NvCN by the ultrasonic method and were tightly combined with CFOv/NvCN.

Through DFT theoretical calculations, the charge transfer behavior of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN was investigated. Fig. S12a shows the optimized structure of NvCN. From Fig. 8 a, it can be observed that the introduction of N vacancies leads to a redistribution of charge density in NvCN, resulting in the formation of electron-rich regions. This charge accumulation causes an upward shift in the conduction and valence band positions of NvCN, inducing the spatial separation of photogenerated charges. Consequently, oxidation and reduction sites in the material are effectively isolated, suppressing the recombination of photogenerated charges and significantly enhancing the activity of catalytic oxidation and reduction reactions [70]. Fig. S12b illustrates the optimized structure of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. The charge density difference of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, as shown in Fig. 8b, primarily occurs at the interface between NvCN and CFOv, indicating that photogenerated electrons can transfer from NvCN to CFOv within the heterojunction [71]. Additionally, electron accumulation and dissipation are observed around N-CQDs, suggesting that N-CQDs can serve as centers for capturing photogenerated electrons, thereby facilitating the separation of photogenerated charge carriers [31].

The mechanism of TC degradation via the photo-Fenton process was elucidated through radical trapping experiments. Isopropanol (IPA) served as ·OH scavenger, EDTA-2Na as h+ scavenger, L-Ascorbic acid as ·O2 − scavenger, and L-histidine as 1O2 scavenger [72]. The scavenger concentrations were maintained at 5 mM. Fig. 9 a illustrates the degradation efficiency of TC in the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN photo-Fenton system. Upon addition of the scavengers to the TC solution, the efficiency of TC degradation decreased from 85.69 % to 83.53 %, 23.30 %, 47.85 %, 67.48 %, and 88.37 %, respectively. The contribution rates of the four active species were evaluated and normalized [73]. As depicted in Fig. 9b, the contribution rates of the four active species, from highest to lowest, were h+ (43.38 %) > ·O2 − (35.04 %) > 1O2 (16.74 %) > ·OH (4.84 %). These results indicate that the primary active species responsible for TC degradation in the photo-Fenton process are h+ and ·O2 −, with 1O2 also playing a significant role, suggesting its involvement as a non-radical reactive oxygen species in TC degradation. Fig. S13 shows the results of the free radical capture experiment under photocatalytic conditions, revealing that the contribution rates of active species are from large to small in the order of ·O2 − (57.22 %) > h+ (18.95 %) > 1O2 (17.22 %) > ·OH (6.60 %). In the free radical capture experiment under photo-Fenton conditions, h+ is the main active species, which may be due to the introduction of H2O2 into the photo-Fenton system, which can capture electrons in the system, and Fe3+ and Cu2+ can also capture photogenerated electrons, promoting the rapid transfer of charges and accelerating the generation of e− and h+.

The generation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) was investigated through coumarin experiments, where a coumarin probe was employed to react with ·OH, yielding 7-hydroxycoumarin. This compound exhibits a fluorescent signal at approximately 456 nm, allowing for the determination of ·OH quantity based on fluorescence intensity [74]. By comparing the fluorescence intensity of different samples, Fig. S14 demonstrates that the production of ·OH in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN is significantly higher than that in CFOv/NvCN and NvCN alone. Furthermore, Fig. S15 compares the generation of ·OH in different systems, revealing that the Photo-Fenton system exhibits the highest production of ·OH, indicating the superior catalytic activity of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN in the Photo-Fenton system. Fig. S16 illustrates that the production of ·OH increases with reaction time.

EPR further confirmed the formation of reactive species in the Photo-Fenton system. In Fig. 9(c-e), characteristic peaks of 4 DMPO-·O2 −, 3 TEMP-1O2, and 4 DMPO-·OH were observed in the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN system, demonstrating the generation of ·O2 −, 1O2, and ·OH in the system. Under dark conditions, no signals for DMPO-·O2 −, TEMP-1O2, or DMPO-·OH were detected in the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN system.

To further investigate the electron transfer in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, changes in the oxidation states of Fe and Cu elements before and after the reaction were explored by comparing the XPS spectra of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. As shown in Fig. S17a, characteristic peaks of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN were still observed after the photo-Fenton reaction, indicating the coexistence of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in the material and confirming its reusability. The ratio of Fe3+/Fe2+ in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN decreased from 0.95 to 0.67, suggesting that the heterostructure effectively promoted the regeneration of Fe2+ and the Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle. Similarly, in Fig. S17b, characteristic peaks belonging to Cu2+ and Cu+ were also observed in the Cu 2p spectra of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN before and after use, with a decrease in the Cu2+/Cu+ ratio from 1.30 to 0.85. These results demonstrate the participation of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in the photo-Fenton reaction of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, wherein they are reduced to Fe2+ and Cu+ by electrons, respectively. This electron-mediated reduction process contributes to maintaining the structural stability of the material, thereby extending its catalytic applicability under different pH conditions [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed TC intermediate degradation pathways and toxicity estimation
ce:section.ce:para: Through 3D EEMs, further investigation into the degradation of TC was conducted. Due to TC's electron-absorbing structure, no significant PL peak was observed in the initial TC solution in Fig. 10 a [75]. After 5 min of degradation, a fluorescence peak at EX/EM 230–255/345–390 was observed in region II (Fig. 10b), attributed to the degradation of TC into aromatic proteins [76]. As shown in Fig. 10c after 10 min of degradation, the intensity of the fluorescence peak increased from 167.54 to 187.56. With reaction times of 15 and 20 min, the fluorescence peak intensity continued to increase in Fig. 10d and Fig. 10e, respectively. As the reaction time further increased to 30 min, the fluorescence intensity significantly increased, reaching 200.70 in Fig. 10f. This outcome indicates the oxidation of TC in the photo-Fenton degradation reaction, leading to the production of smaller molecular intermediate products with stronger fluorescence intensity.

In the wavelength range, the synchronous fluorescence intensity increases with the degradation time (Fig. S18). The synchronous and asynchronous fluorescence 2D correlation spectroscopy (2DCOS) are depicted in Fig. S19. In Fig. S19a, two prominent autocorrelation peaks are observed distributed along the diagonal of the synchronous plot, located at 390–420 nm and 470–490 nm, exhibiting higher intensity. The autocorrelation peak at 470–490 nm displays higher intensity and is more sensitive to the entire reaction system.

In the asynchronous plot of Fig. S19b, negative cross-correlation peaks (280 nm, 480 nm) are observed. By analyzing the sign of the cross-correlation peaks [77], it is inferred that the fluorescence changes occur in the order of 280 nm > 480 nm. Combining the results from 3D EEMs, it is deduced that the changes in proteinaceous substances precede those of humic acid-like substances. The 2DCOS results further reveal the sequential structural changes in the fluorescence components of TC during the photo-Fenton degradation process, providing crucial insights into the degradation dynamics of different organic compounds during TC degradation.

DFT theoretical calculations were employed to explore potential degradation pathways of TC. In order to predict reaction sites, Fukui functions were computed using the Gaussian 16 software package. Sites with higher f−, f+, and f0 values in the Fukui function exhibit greater reactivity, representing their susceptibility to electrophilic, nucleophilic, and radical attacks, respectively [78]. Fig. 11 a illustrates the optimized TC model and isosurface plots of Fukui functions (f−, f+, and f0). Computed Fukui functions, condensed, are depicted in Table S4. Based on the computational simulations, the major reactive species, h+ and ·O2 −, representing electrophilic and nucleophilic attacks, respectively, were identified from free radical capture experiments, while 1O2 and ·OH, representing electrophilic and radical attacks, were considered minor reactive species. Hence, in elucidating the degradation pathways of TC, the influences of electrophilic, nucleophilic, and radical attacks should be taken into account.

Additionally, intermediate products of TC degradation were obtained via liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). The detected intermediate products along with their molecular structures are presented in Table S5. By combining the results of Fukui function analysis and LC-MS, three potential degradation pathways of TC were inferred.

As shown in Fig. 11b, in Pathway 1, O24, O25, C9, and C8 exhibit relatively high f− values (0.0559, 0.076, 0.0634, and 0.0469, respectively) and f0 values (0.0402, 0.0706, 0.0398, and 0.0353, respectively), making them susceptible to attacks by h+, 1O2, and ·OH. C12 possesses a high f+ value (0.0473) and f0 value (0.0337), making it prone to attacks by ·O2 − and ·OH, leading to the occurrence of ring-opening reactions and elimination reactions, resulting in the dehydrogenation and de-aldehyde of TC to yield product A1. Moreover, C16 with a high f− value (0.0379) and f0 value (0.029), C18 with a high f+ value (0.0484) and f0 value (0.0435), and C20 with relatively high f− (0.0267) and f+ (0.0303) values are more susceptible to attacks by active species, leading to ring-opening reactions and the formation of product A2. Subsequently, A2 undergoes further ring-opening reactions and hydroxyl group removal to generate the product A3. A3 further undergoes ring-opening and addition reactions to produce product A4. Under the attack of active species, A4 undergoes ring-opening reactions to yield the product A5.

In Pathway 2, N32 exhibits relatively high f− (0.0288) and f0 (0.0214) values, making it susceptible to attacks by h+, 1O2, and ·OH. Meanwhile, C5 and C6 possess higher f+ values (0.0587 and 0.0224, respectively) and f0 values (0.0312 and 0.0156, respectively), rendering them more prone to attacks by ·O2 − and ·OH. This results in the ring-opening reactions of TC and the removal of amino groups to generate product B1. Additionally, due to the relatively high f+ values (0.0622 and 0.0224) and f0 values (0.0369 and 0.0156) of C1 and C6, it indicates that the C=C bonds are more susceptible to attacks by ·O2 − and ·OH. Consequently, B1 undergoes ring-opening reactions to yield B2. Subsequently, B2 undergoes further ring-opening and reduction reactions to generate B3. B3 undergoes ring-opening reactions and hydroxyl group removal to yield B4. Under the continuous attack of active species, B4 undergoes a series of ring-opening reactions to yield the products B5 and B6.

In Pathway 3, C16 and C18 exhibit relatively high f− values (0.0379 and 0.0386, respectively) and f0 values (0.029 and 0.0435, respectively), making them susceptible to attacks by h+, 1O2, and ·OH. Additionally, C20 has a relatively high f− value (0.0267) and f+ value (0.0303), rendering it susceptible to attacks by h+, 1O2, and ·O2 −. Consequently, TC undergoes ring-opening reactions to generate the product C1. Furthermore, C15 and C17 have relatively high f− values (0.0204 and 0.0278, respectively) and f0 values (0.0173 and 0.0233, respectively). Upon attacks by h+, 1O2, and ·OH, C1 undergoes ring-opening reactions to yield the product C2. Subsequently, C2 undergoes further ring-opening reactions to yield the small-molecule products C3, C4, and C5. Finally, under the continuous attack of active species, these intermediate products are mineralized into CO2, H2O, and NH4 +.

The acute and chronic toxicities, bioaccumulation factor, developmental toxicity, and mutagenicity of TC and its degradation intermediates were assessed through QASR analysis using toxicity assessment software (ECOSAR and TEST)[79]. The evaluation results are presented in Tables S6 and S7. As shown in Fig. S20 (a-c), concerning acute toxicity, the toxicity increased when TC degraded into larger molecular products initially; however, with the continued degradation reaction, the toxicity levels of the generated intermediates decreased to varying degrees. Ultimately, the products A5, B6, and C6 exhibited significantly reduced toxicity compared to the initial TC molecule. Fig. S20 (d-f) demonstrates a similar trend in chronic toxicity for TC and its intermediates. Fig. S20g indicates the bioaccumulation factor of TC and its degradation intermediates, where some intermediates, such as B3, B5, B6, C2, C3, C5, and C6, displayed higher bioaccumulation factors than TC (0.71). These intermediates may potentially accumulate through the food chain, posing potential risks to human health (data for the bioaccumulation factor of A1, A2, A3, B1, B2, and B4 were not available in TEST). Fig. S20h illustrates the developmental toxicity of TC during degradation, showing that all intermediates exhibited lower developmental toxicity compared to TC, with B5, B6, and C6 even demonstrating no toxicity. Regarding mutagenicity, as observed in Fig. S20i, most intermediates showed negative mutagenicity as the degradation process occurred. Based on the aforementioned toxicity assessment results, the toxicity of TC was significantly reduced after undergoing the N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN photo-Fenton reaction.

The optical absorption properties of semiconductors are crucial factors determining their catalytic activity. UV–visible spectroscopy was employed to investigate the light absorption properties of NvCN, CFOv, CFOv/NvCN, and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. As shown in Fig. 12 a, NvCN exhibits an absorption edge at around 442 nm, while CFOv demonstrates extensive light absorption capability in the visible range. On the composite of NvCN and CFOv, the absorption edge of CFOv/NvCN notably red-shifts. Upon the introduction of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN exhibit a slight red-shift and strong visible-light response, indicating that N-CQDs enhance the light absorption capacity of CFOv/NvCN, thereby inducing the generation of more photoexcited charge carriers [80].

The bandgaps of NvCN and CFOv were estimated using the Tauc equation (Eq. (10), as shown in Fig. 12b. Wherein, α is the molar absorption coefficient, h is Planck constant (h = 4.1356676969 × 10−15 eV s), v is the incident photon frequency, hv is the photon energy, A is the proportional constant, Eg is the optical band gap of the semiconductor material, and the value of n is related to the semiconductor material. When n = 4, it corresponds to an indirect band gap semiconductor material; when n = 1, it corresponds to a direct band gap semiconductor material. The calculated values for the bandgaps (Eg) of NvCN and CFOv are 2.70 eV and 1.67 eV, respectively. Based on the XPS-VB valence band spectra in Fig. 12c, the valence bands of NvCN and CFOv can be approximated to be estimated at 1.98 eV and 0.48 eV, respectively.

The conduction band edges of NvCN and CFOv were evaluated using Mott-Schottky curves. As shown in Fig. 12d, the Efb (flat band potential) of NvCN and CFOv relative to the saturated calomel electrode were −0.87 eV and −1.32 eV, respectively. The positive slope of the curve indicates that both NvCN and CFOv are n-type semiconductors. By calculating using Eq. (11) referenced to the standard hydrogen electrode, the conduction band edge positions of NvCN and CFOv were determined to be −0.63 eV and −1.08 eV, respectively (Where ENHE is the standard hydrogen electrode potential and ESCE is the saturated calomel electrode potential).

Generally, the Efb of n-type semiconductors is higher than the ECB by 0.1 ∼ 0.3 eV [39]. The ECB values for NvCN and CFOv were calculated to be −0.73 eV and −1.18 eV, respectively, which are close to the reported values in the literature [81]. According to the equation EVB=ECB+Eg, the EVB values were calculated to be 1.97 eV and 0.49 eV, respectively, which are consistent with the data from XPS to VB. Combining the above results, the band structure diagrams of NvCN and CFOv are shown in Fig. 12e, where an alternating arrangement of energy levels between NvCN and CFOv is observed. The combination of the two provides the possibility for the construction of a Z-type heterojunction.

EIS was employed to investigate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [82]. Fig. 12f illustrates that N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN displays the smallest arc radius relative to NvCN, CFOv, and CFOv/NvCN. This observation indicates that the charge transfer in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN is significantly enhanced [16,83]. This is advantageous for the generation of ·OH in N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN, thereby facilitating the reduction reaction of Fe3+. The accelerated electron transfer enhances the rate of Fe2+ formation, which can more effectively activate H2O2 and generate ∙OH to participate in the photo-Fenton reaction, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity [84]. PL spectroscopy was employed to investigate the separation and migration capabilities of photogenerated charge carriers in NvCN, CFOv/NvCN, and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. Generally speaking, a higher emission peak means that photogenerated e− and h+ are easier to combine, and the catalytic activity is lower [85]. It can be observed from Fig. 12g that NvCN and CFOv exhibit higher emission intensities, indicating that their photogenerated charge carrier recombination rates are high. When CFOv is combined with NvCN, the emission signal is weakened compared to NvCN alone, indicating that the introduction of CFOv improves the issue of easier recombination of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) in NvCN. Additionally, it is observed that the emission peak intensity of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN is the lowest, suggesting effective suppression of photogenerated charge carrier recombination, thereby enhancing catalytic performance.

In Fig. S21, N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN exhibits a larger loop in the cyclic voltammetry test compared to NvCN, CFOv, and CFOv/NvCN. This observation suggests that N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN achieve higher photocurrent density and superior charge separation capability [36], consistent with the findings from the EIS test.

The improvement in interfacial transport is further demonstrated by time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy and transient photocurrent response. As shown in Fig. 12h, the average electron lifetime of CFOv/NvCN is 24.64 ns, indicating that the introduction of CFOv effectively suppresses the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. The average electron lifetime of N-CQDs/CFOvCFOv/NvCN is 25.27 ns, indicating that the loading of N-CQDs and CFOv improves the charge carrier transport rate and enhances the catalytic activity. Fig. 12i shows that N-CQDs/CFOvCFOv/NvCN shows higher photocurrents than CFOv/NvCN, CFOv, and NvCN, indicating that the formation of the Z-type heterojunction effectively enhances the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers.

In order to better prove the above analysis, the in situ XPS spectra of CFOv/NvCN and N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN were tested in a dark environment and under illumination, respectively, and are shown in Figs. S22 and 13. In general, the left shift of the peak corresponds to an increase in binding energy and represents the loss of electrons, while the right shift indicates a decrease in binding energy and an increase in electrons. In Fig. S22 (a-b), the C 1 s and N 1 s peaks of CFOv/NvCN move to the left, indicating that electrons flow out of NvCN, while in Fig. S22 (c-e), the O 1 s, Fe 2p, and Cu 2p peaks of CFOv/NvCN are all observed to move to the right, indicating that electrons flow out of CFOv. Fig. 13 (a-b) shows that after the introduction of N-CQDs, the C 1 s and N 1 s peaks of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN show a shift to the left and right. The above results can infer that there is electron inflow and outflow in N-CQDs, proving the electron transfer path and the formation of Z-Scheme heterojunction.

Based on the experimental results presented above, the mechanism of TC degradation by the Z-scheme N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN heterojunction photocatalyst is proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 14 . Under visible light irradiation, e− and h+ are generated in NvCN and CFOv, respectively (Eqs. 12–13). N-CQDs, acting as electron acceptors, facilitate electron transfer, allowing low-reduction e- from the CB of NvCN to transfer to CFOv through N-CQDs, where they combine with low-oxidation h+ from the VB of CFOv. This process effectively preserves the high reduction e− in CFOv and the high oxidation h+ in NvCN, suppressing the recombination of photoinduced charge carriers and retaining the maximum oxidation and reduction ability [86]. The high-reduction e− in CFOv can react with O2 to generate ·O2 − (Eq. (14). Since the VB of NvCN (1.97 eV) is more negative than the potential for OH− oxidation to ·OH (1.99 eV vs. NHE), ·OH is more likely to be generated through other pathways. With the introduction of H2O2, H2O2 can capture e− in the system, generating ·OH in this process (Eq. (15). The reaction of H2O2 and ·OH can further produce ·O2 − (Eqs. 16–17). Fe2+ on the surface of CFOv can activate H2O2 to produce ·OH, while a small amount of Fe3+ in CFOv is reduced to Fe2+ by H2O2 (Eqs. 18–19). Similarly, ·OH can also be generated from the reaction between Cu+ in CFOv and H2O2 (Eq. (20). Fe3+ and Cu2+ can capture photogenerated electrons, facilitating rapid charge transfer and accelerating the cycle of Fe3+/Fe2+ and Cu2+/Cu+ (Eqs. 21–23). Meanwhile, oxygen vacancies on the surface of CFOv provide active sites for the activation of H2O2, accelerating the formation of ·OH and enhancing catalytic activity (Eqs. 24–25) [86]. According to the results of free radical capture, it is inferred that non-free radical 1O2 may exist in the system, which may be produced by the reaction between a large amount of ·O2 − and H2O and ·OH in the system (Eqs. 26–27) [17]. Finally, under the continuous attack of active species, TC is fully degraded and mineralized (Eq. (28).

Degraded products

This study successfully prepared N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN composites through simple thermal polymerization and in-situ deposition processes. The appropriate band coupling between CFOv and NvCN allows for kinetically driven spatial charge separation and transfer. The introduction of CFOv not only enhances the magnetic separation performance and photothermal effect of the photocatalyst but also improves its stability. Meanwhile, N-CQDs play a crucial role in the catalytic process by providing efficient channels for charge carrier transfer, thereby achieving rapid spatial charge separation and enhancing light absorption capacity. Photocatalytic tests demonstrate that the optimized N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN exhibit excellent efficiency in photogenerated charge carrier separation. Furthermore, the photo-Fenton system of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN shows strong resistance to ion interference and does not require adjustment of solution pH, achieving good degradation performance under different water quality conditions. The stability of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN was verified through cyclic experiments and related characterizations, and it was found that the biological toxicity of the solution was significantly reduced after degradation. The formation of the Z-scheme heterojunction significantly enhances the redox ability of N-CQDs/CFOv/NvCN. This study lays a solid foundation for the application of Z-scheme photocatalysts in environmental remediation. Based on DFT theoretical calculations and mass spectrometry analysis, potential TC degradation mechanisms were derived. In summary, this catalyst demonstrates great potential for treating actual antibiotic wastewater.

Lanhe Zhang: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Haina Bai: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology.
